real_meaning: ～てもいい expresses permission — either granting it ('you may do X') or requesting it ('may I do X?'). The core feeling is that doing X falls within an acceptable range; nothing bad happens if it occurs. In questions, it carries a polite, slightly tentative tone — the speaker is checking whether crossing into that action is okay with the listener.

formation.rule: Attach もいい to the て-form of a verb. Works the same for all verb types: <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べる → <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べてもいい, <ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>む → <ruby>飲<rt>の</rt></ruby>んでもいい, する → してもいい, くる → きてもいい. The て-form's voiced ending (で) is kept when required.
formation.common_mistake: Using the dictionary form instead of the て-form: ✗ <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べるもいい → ✓ <ruby>食<rt>た</rt></ruby>べてもいい

sentences.jp: ここに<ruby>座<rt>すわ</rt></ruby>ってもいい？
sentences.notes: The rising intonation on いい turns this into a casual permission request — dropping ですか makes it feel natural between friends or strangers in informal settings.

sentences.jp: レポートは<ruby>明日<rt>あした</rt></ruby><ruby>出<rt>だ</rt></ruby>してもいいですよ。
sentences.translation: You can hand in the report tomorrow.
sentences.notes: Adding よ at the end softens the grant of permission and makes it sound reassuring rather than just procedural.

sentences.jp: <ruby>会議<rt>かいぎ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>資料<rt>しりょう</rt></ruby>をコピーしてもよろしいでしょうか。
sentences.translation: Would it be alright if I made copies of the meeting materials?
sentences.notes: In formal contexts, いい is replaced with よろしい and か becomes でしょうか — this is the polished, professional version of the same permission-request structure.

sentences.jp: <ruby>別<rt>べつ</rt></ruby>に<ruby>来<rt>こ</rt></ruby>なくてもいいよ。
sentences.translation: You don't have to come, honestly.
sentences.notes: The negative て-form (〜なくて) + もいい means 'it's fine NOT to do X' — this is the tricky flip side that trips learners up; it grants permission to *not* act, not permission to act.

confused_with.contrast: Both express permission, but they differ in tone. ～てもいい is warm and everyday; ～てもかまわない literally means 'I don't mind if you do X' and carries a more neutral, sometimes slightly reluctant or indifferent feel. Compare: <ruby>帰<rt>かえ</rt></ruby>ってもいいよ ('You can go home — go ahead!') vs. <ruby>帰<rt>かえ</rt></ruby>ってもかまわない ('I don't mind if you go home' — neutral, maybe even a little cold). Use ～てもいい when you want to sound warm or encouraging; use ～てもかまわない in formal writing or when you're being deliberately neutral.

bunpro_tip: Picture a green traffic light: もいい = the light is green for that action, it's in the OK zone. When drilling on BunPro, always check whether the sentence is *granting* or *requesting* permission — both use the exact same form, so context (statement vs. question) is your only clue.
